nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams and flooded forest margins of the upper Amazon basin; soft muddy or sandy substrates with leaf litter, often under overhanging vegetation or among submerged roots

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (sharp gravel risks barbel damage)
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak) for tannins and hiding cover
low to moderate planting (Java fern, Anubias, hornwort)

diet.notes: Opportunistic bottom forager. Accepts high-quality sinking pellets, wafers, and granules formulated for catfish. Relishes frozen and live foods: bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, brine shrimp, and micro-worms. Also takes blanched vegetables. Should not be expected to survive on leftover food from tank inhabitants — target-feed sinking foods after lights dim.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (neons, cardinals, ember tetras)
avoidWith: large barbs known to nip fins
any fish large enough to swallow them

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Corydoras leucomelas; shows typical adult spotted pattern and fin coloration.

summary: A small, sociable armoured catfish from the upper Amazon basin, closely resembling C. punctatus and C. ambiacus. Hardy and easy to keep in groups of 6+; tolerates a range of water conditions but thrives in softer, slightly acidic water. Pectoral spines are venomous — handle with care.
careNotes:
The False Spotted Catfish is one of the more robust and adaptable corydoras, making it an excellent choice for community aquariums housing small peaceful species. A group of at least six individuals is strongly recommended — these catfish are highly social and display far more natural foraging behaviour in numbers, often moving together across the substrate in a coordinated troop. A group of 10–15 produces a particularly attractive display.

Fine sand is the preferred substrate; sharp gravel may abrade barbels, and deteriorating barbels impair the fish's ability to locate food. A minimum substrate depth of ~3 cm allows natural rooting behaviour. Leaf litter (Indian almond, oak leaves) is appreciated and contributes tannins consistent with the blackwater biotope. Filtration should provide gentle to moderate flow; the species dislikes strong current.

Water quality must be maintained diligently — corydoras are sensitive to elevated ammonia and nitrite. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Temperature is best kept between 22–26°C; slightly cooler water (down ~2°C) is commonly used to trigger spawning.

Caution: the stiffened pectoral-fin spines are capable of piercing skin and can deliver a mildly venomous sting from axillary gland secretions at the spine base. Avoid hand-netting; use a container or soft mesh net.

This species is readily confused with C. punctatus (Punctatus Cory) and C. ambiacus; C. leucomelas can be distinguished by its strongly pronounced eye band, the dark pigmentation of the dorsal fin (including a large black spot at the leading edge), and a distinct vertical black band at the caudal peduncle. The 2024 Dias et al. phylogenomic revision (Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society) reclassified C. leucomelas to Hoplisoma leucomelas; the hobby name Corydoras leucomelas remains in widespread use and the new name has not yet been universally adopted by retailers or aquarium literature.
breedingNotes: Spawning can be triggered by a partial water change (50–70%) with slightly cooler water combined with increased surface agitation and oxygenation. Condition breeders with live or frozen foods for several weeks beforehand. Use a ratio of 2–3 males per female. Eggs are deposited on plant leaves, spawning mops, or glass surfaces and should be removed to a separate rearing container to prevent predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 24–26°C. Fry require infusoria or micro-worm for the first few days, transitioning to Artemia nauplii and finely crushed flake. Raising fry over a thin sand layer (rather than bare-bottom) is reported to improve early survival. Breeding difficulty is moderate — the spawning trigger is straightforward, but fry require attentive care and excellent water quality.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras leucomelas
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided type locality (Yarinacocha, Peru), tank dimensions (90×30 cm minimum), spawning methodology, confusion species (C. agassizii, C. ambiacus, C. gomezi), venomous spine warning, and fry husbandry. Water parameter fields were not fully populated in the live profile; supplemented from FishBase and petmedaily.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras leucomelas
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (4.5 cm SL), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), native range (upper Amazon, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–8.0, dH 2–25, demersal habitat, facultative air-breathing. Species listed as Hoplisoma leucomelas following 2024 revision.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cory Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General corydoras care guide. Confirmed group size recommendation (6+), pH 6.5–7.8, temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), fine sand or smooth gravel substrate, sinking food feeding strategy, and venomous spine handling warning. No dedicated C. leucomelas page exists.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Corydoras leucomelas
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Peru type locality Yarina Cocha, also Colombia, Bolivia, Ecuador), adult size 5–5.5 cm, schooling behavior, and recommended group size of 10–15 for best display. Also referenced the 2024 Dias et al. reclassification placing leucomelas in Hoplisoma.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoradinae revision changes names for hundreds of species
additional.notes: Covered the Dias et al. (2024) Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society phylogenomic revision reclassifying the Corydoradinae subfamily; confirmed Corydoras leucomelas moved to Hoplisoma leucomelas under the new classification.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning triggered by cool-water changes (50–70%) and increased oxygenation. 2–3 males per female; eggs laid on plants or glass. Hatch 3–4 days; fry need micro-worm then Artemia nauplii. Fry care requires good water quality and attentive feeding.
